definition: The invisible mixture of gases that surrounds the Earth and that we breathe.

meaning: ठीक है / सब ठीक (theek hai / sab theek)
definition: Similar to okay; meaning acceptable or in good condition.
Greatly surprised; astonished.
Finding something funny or entertaining.
The upper limb of the body, from the shoulder to the hand.

meaning: Textbook: Requires a verb to complete the clause (e.g., 'As far as I know'). Spoken Native: In spoken English, often followed immediately by a noun phrase to introduce a topic (e.g., 'As far as the budget...').
definition: Textbook: Requires a verb to complete the clause (e.g., 'As far as I know'). Spoken Native: In spoken English, often followed immediately by a noun phrase to introduce a topic (e.g., 'As far as the budget...').
example: As far as public transit, the city is doing a great job.

definition: The rear part of the human body from the shoulders to the hips.
Of poor quality or low standard; unacceptable.
example: I need a vacation so bad; I'm completely burnt out.

definition: A room with a toilet and usually a sink and a bath or shower.

meaning: Textbook: Past participle of 'be.' Spoken Native: In African American Vernacular English (AAVE) and spoken slang, 'been' (stressed) is used to mean something started in the far past and is still true.
definition: Textbook: Past participle of 'be.' Spoken Native: In African American Vernacular English (AAVE) and spoken slang, 'been' (stressed) is used to mean something started in the far past and is still true.
example: I been known about that movie; it's a classic.

definition: The hard white pieces that form the skeleton of the body.
A set of printed pages bound together inside a cover.
A person who is in charge of a worker or organization.
A container with a narrow neck, used for holding liquids.
To fail to do what one said they would do.
example: She never forgave him for breaking a promise.

definition: To do something illegal.
example: If you drive too fast, you are breaking the law.

definition: To be the first to tell someone some important information.
example: I'm sorry to be the one to break the news.

definition: To do something that is not allowed.
example: If you break the rules, you will be penalized.

meaning: Textbook: Expresses capability or physical ability. Spoken Native: Used interchangeably with 'may' to request or grant permission in casual and business environments.
definition: Textbook: Expresses capability or physical ability. Spoken Native: Used interchangeably with 'may' to request or grant permission in casual and business environments.
example: Can I leave the meeting early today?

meaning: ख्याल रखने वाला, परवाह करने वाला
definition: Displaying kindness and concern for others.
To get on a bus before it leaves.
example: I have to leave now to catch a bus.

definition: To arrive at the station in time to board the train.
example: Hurry up, or we won't catch our train.

definition: To begin to burn.
example: Dry grass can easily catch fire in the summer.

definition: To attract someone's attention.
example: The colorful painting immediately caught my eye.

definition: The front of the body between the neck and the stomach.
Things such as shirts and trousers that you wear on your body.
A person that you work with in a profession or business.
To almost achieve or happen.
example: We didn't win, but we came very close.

definition: To be the most important priority, or to win a race.
example: In this company, safety always comes first.

definition: To arrive precisely at the scheduled time.
example: Please make sure you come on time for the meeting.

definition: To reach a mutual decision.
example: After hours of discussion, they finally came to an agreement.

definition: To finish or conclude.
example: All good things must come to an end.

meaning: पूरी तरह से / मुकम्मल तौर पर (poori tarah se)
definition: In every way or as much as possible.
An electronic machine that can store and work with large amounts of information.
Unable to understand or think clearly.

meaning: Textbook: Past ability of 'can.' Spoken Native: Frequently used to make tentative suggestions or polite requests, sounding less direct than 'can'.
definition: Textbook: Past ability of 'can.' Spoken Native: Frequently used to make tentative suggestions or polite requests, sounding less direct than 'can'.
example: Could we possibly move the meeting to Wednesday?

meaning: Textbook: Criticized as logically meaning you care some. Spoken Native: Used by many native speakers to mean 'don't care at all' (synonymous with 'couldn't care less').
definition: Textbook: Criticized as logically meaning you care some. Spoken Native: Used by many native speakers to mean 'don't care at all' (synonymous with 'couldn't care less').
example: I could care less about what they think of my outfit.
I'll be gone for a couple of minutes to grab some water.

meaning: Textbook: Vulgar term for feces. Spoken Native: Used as a mild expletive or to describe cheap items, lies, nonsense, or poor quality (e.g., 'that's a load of crap').
definition: Textbook: Vulgar term for feces. Spoken Native: Used as a mild expletive or to describe cheap items, lies, nonsense, or poor quality (e.g., 'that's a load of crap').
example: My old laptop is absolute crap; it crashes every hour.

definition: The time between sunrise and sunset.
The end of life.
In a state of general unhappiness or despondency.

meaning: Textbook: Recommends 'different from.' Spoken Native: 'Different than' is very common and natural in spoken English, particularly in American English when followed by a full clause.
definition: Textbook: Recommends 'different from.' Spoken Native: 'Different than' is very common and natural in spoken English, particularly in American English when followed by a full clause.
example: The new job is different than I expected.

definition: Sad or displeased because someone or something has failed to fulfill one's hopes or expectations.
To perform a helpful or kind act for someone.
example: Could you do me a favor and hold the door?

definition: To trade or have commercial dealings.
example: It is a pleasure doing business with you.

definition: To cause harm or destruction.
example: The heavy rain did a lot of damage to the crops.

definition: To prepare food.
example: My husband usually does the cooking in our house.

definition: To wash the dirty clothes.
example: I usually do the laundry on Sunday mornings.

definition: To try as hard as you can.
example: As long as you do your best, we will be proud.

definition: A person whose job is to treat people who are sick or injured.
A piece of wood, glass, or metal that you open and close to enter a room or building.

meaning: Textbook: Singular determiner. Spoken Native: Often paired with plural pronouns (they/them/their) in speech to avoid gender-biased language like 'his or her.'
definition: Textbook: Singular determiner. Spoken Native: Often paired with plural pronouns (they/them/their) in speech to avoid gender-biased language like 'his or her.'
example: Each student must turn in their assignment by Monday.

definition: The organ used for hearing on the sides of the head.
A person who is actively opposed or hostile to someone.
The latter part of the day, before night.

meaning: Textbook: Singular pronoun requiring singular verbs and pronouns. Spoken Native: Although paired with singular verbs, it is almost always referred to with plural pronouns (they/them/their) to maintain gender neutrality.
definition: Textbook: Singular pronoun requiring singular verbs and pronouns. Spoken Native: Although paired with singular verbs, it is almost always referred to with plural pronouns (they/them/their) to maintain gender neutrality.
example: Everyone has their own way of doing things.

meaning: बुरी तरह थका हुआ (Buri tarah thaka hua)
definition: Very tired.
Costing a lot of money.
The organ of the body that gives you the ability to see.
Not genuine; pretending to be something one is not.
A machine with blades that turn to move air and make you feel cooler.

meaning: Textbook: Used only with countable nouns. Spoken Native: Replaced by 'less' in almost all casual and spoken contexts (e.g., 'less people' instead of 'fewer people').
definition: Textbook: Used only with countable nouns. Spoken Native: Replaced by 'less' in almost all casual and spoken contexts (e.g., 'less people' instead of 'fewer people').
example: There are less cars on the road today than yesterday.

definition: One of the five separate parts at the end of the hand.
The state of burning that produces flames, heat, and light.
The flat surface of a room on which you walk.
The part of the leg that touches the ground when you stand.

meaning: Textbook: For all future time; eternally. Spoken Native: Used hyperbolically to mean a very long or boring period of time.
definition: Textbook: For all future time; eternally. Spoken Native: Used hyperbolically to mean a very long or boring period of time.
example: It took forever for the bus to arrive this morning.

definition: Kind and pleasant towards others.
The forward part or surface of something.

meaning: निराश और क्रोधित / कुंठित (Niraash aur krodhit / Kunthit)
definition: Feeling annoyed or less confident because you cannot achieve what you want.
The time that will come after the present.
A piece of land next to a house, used for growing flowers, fruit, or vegetables.
To secure employment.
example: After graduating, her main goal was to get a job.

definition: To become mad or furious.
example: He tends to get angry when things go wrong.

definition: To prepare for something.
example: Give me five minutes to get ready.

definition: To become unhappy, disappointed, or worried.
example: Don't get upset over such a small mistake.

definition: To allow someone to do something.
example: The principal gave us permission to leave early.

definition: To travel to a foreign country.
example: They plan to go abroad for their honeymoon.

definition: To become unable to pay one's debts.
example: The company will go bankrupt if sales don't improve.

definition: To lose one's eyesight completely.
example: The disease can cause people to go blind.

definition: To disappear and not be found.
example: The dog went missing after the storm.

definition: To connect to the internet.
example: You need to go online to register for the course.

definition: To be desired or approved of; of high quality.
example: She plays the piano really good for her age.

meaning: Textbook: Informal slang contraction of 'have got to.' Spoken Native: Extremely common in daily spoken English to express necessity or an urgent task.
definition: Informal spoken reduction of 'have got to' or 'got to', meaning 'have to' or 'must'.
example: I gotta run or I'll be late for my appointment.

definition: A person who is invited to visit someone's home or a place.

meaning: Textbook: Plural noun meaning males. Spoken Native: Used as a gender-neutral plural pronoun to address any group of two or more people.
definition: Textbook: Plural noun meaning males. Spoken Native: Used as a gender-neutral plural pronoun to address any group of two or more people.
example: What do you guys want to eat for dinner tonight?

definition: The thread-like strands growing on a person's head or body.
Putting a lot of effort and care into work.
A very strong feeling of dislike.
To give birth to a child.
example: They are planning to have a baby next year.

definition: To wash oneself in a bathtub.
example: I like to have a hot bath before bed.

definition: To enjoy oneself.
example: We went to the beach and had a good time.

definition: To encounter a difficulty or issue.
example: If you have a problem, just ask for help.

definition: To stop working or moving to relax.
example: You look tired; you should have a rest.

meaning: Textbook: Criticized as redundant. Spoken Native: The standard spoken expression for possession or obligation in both British and American English (e.g., 'I've got a headache').
definition: Textbook: Criticized as redundant. Spoken Native: The standard spoken expression for possession or obligation in both British and American English (e.g., 'I've got a headache').
example: I have got to get some sleep tonight.

meaning: Textbook: Semi-modal of external obligation. Spoken Native: The default spoken alternative to 'must' for expressing everyday obligations and rules.
definition: Textbook: Semi-modal of external obligation. Spoken Native: The default spoken alternative to 'must' for expressing everyday obligations and rules.
example: I have to finish this report by five o'clock.

definition: The condition of a person's body or mind, usually meaning free from illness.

meaning: Textbook: Piles of objects. Spoken Native: In British, Australian, and New Zealand English, used to mean 'a lot' or 'very much' (e.g., 'thanks heaps').
definition: Textbook: Piles of objects. Spoken Native: In British, Australian, and New Zealand English, used to mean 'a lot' or 'very much' (e.g., 'thanks heaps').
example: Don't worry, we've got heaps of time before the train leaves.

definition: The place where one lives permanently.
Telling the truth and not stealing or cheating.
A place where people who are sick or injured are given medical care.
A building for human habitation.
Feeling emotional pain or sadness.

meaning: Textbook: The subjunctive mood for hypothetical statements. Spoken Native: Often replaced by 'If I was' in everyday spoken conversation.
definition: Textbook: The subjunctive mood for hypothetical statements. Spoken Native: Often replaced by 'If I was' in everyday spoken conversation.
example: If I was in your shoes, I would probably accept the job.

definition: To regularly write down one's thoughts and experiences.
example: She has kept a diary since she was ten.

definition: To remain relaxed and not panicked.
example: In an emergency, it is important to keep calm.

definition: To maintain power or authority over something.
example: The teacher struggled to keep control of the noisy class.

definition: To remember something when making a decision.
example: Keep in mind that the store closes early on Sundays.

definition: To remain silent.
example: Please keep quiet while the baby is sleeping.

definition: A room where food is kept, prepared, and cooked.
The joint in the middle of the leg.
Unwilling to work or use energy.
Opposite of right; or past tense of leave.
One of the lower limbs of the body used for walking and standing.
Something that you say that you know is not true.
The condition that distinguishes animals and plants from dead matter; existence.
Giving firm and constant support or allegiance to a person or institution.
To create a dirty or untidy state.
example: The kids made a huge mess in the living room.

definition: To establish that something is true or certain.
example: Make sure you lock the door before you leave.
Can I borrow your pen for a second?

meaning: Textbook: Objective pronoun. Spoken Native: Frequently used as part of a compound subject in casual speech (e.g., 'Me and Sarah' instead of 'Sarah and I').
definition: Textbook: Objective pronoun. Spoken Native: Frequently used as part of a compound subject in casual speech (e.g., 'Me and Sarah' instead of 'Sarah and I').
example: Me and my brother went fishing last weekend.

meaning: Textbook: State of untidiness or dirty. Spoken Native: Used as a noun to describe a person who is disorganized, emotionally unstable, or in a difficult situation.
definition: Textbook: State of untidiness or dirty. Spoken Native: Used as a noun to describe a person who is disorganized, emotionally unstable, or in a difficult situation.
example: After the breakup, she was a complete mess for a few weeks.

definition: The part of a person that thinks, feels, understands, and remembers.
Wretchedly unhappy or uncomfortable.
Coins and banknotes used to buy things.
One of the twelve periods of time a year is divided into.
The early part of the day.
The opening in the face used for eating, speaking, and breathing.

meaning: Textbook: Expresses obligation or strict rule. Spoken Native: Rarely used in speech for obligation (replaced by 'have to' or 'need to'); mostly used to express a logical deduction (e.g., 'You must be tired').
definition: Textbook: Expresses obligation or strict rule. Spoken Native: Rarely used in speech for obligation (replaced by 'have to' or 'need to'); mostly used to express a logical deduction (e.g., 'You must be tired').
example: You've been working all day; you must be exhausted.

meaning: Textbook: Reflexive or intensive pronoun. Spoken Native: Often used incorrectly in place of 'me' or 'I' in professional emails to sound more formal (e.g., 'Please contact myself').
definition: Textbook: Reflexive or intensive pronoun. Spoken Native: Often used incorrectly in place of 'me' or 'I' in professional emails to sound more formal (e.g., 'Please contact myself').
example: If you have any questions, you can email myself or Mark.

definition: The part of the body connecting the head to the shoulders.
A person who lives near or next to you.

meaning: Textbook: District where people live. Spoken Native: Used in the idiom 'in the neighborhood of' to mean approximately or roughly a certain amount or price.
definition: Textbook: District where people live. Spoken Native: Used in the idiom 'in the neighborhood of' to mean approximately or roughly a certain amount or price.
example: The repairs will cost in the neighborhood of five hundred dollars.

meaning: Textbook: Used with singular verbs. Spoken Native: Frequently paired with a plural verb in spoken English when followed by 'of' and a plural noun.
definition: Textbook: Used with singular verbs. Spoken Native: Frequently paired with a plural verb in spoken English when followed by 'of' and a plural noun.
example: Neither of those options are very appealing to me.
I never saw him at the party last night, did he show up?

definition: The time from sunset to sunrise.

meaning: Textbook: Takes a singular verb. Spoken Native: Almost always takes a plural verb in casual speech and writing (e.g., 'None of them are here').
definition: Textbook: Takes a singular verb. Spoken Native: Almost always takes a plural verb in casual speech and writing (e.g., 'None of them are here').
example: None of my friends are going to the concert.

definition: The part of the face used for breathing and smelling.

example: We often visit the beach during the summer months.

definition: The physical feeling caused by disease, injury, or something that hurts.
Feeling sudden, overwhelming fear or terror.
A person who takes part in an undertaking with another, or a spouse.
To pay money as a punishment for an offense.
example: He had to pay a fine for parking illegally.

definition: To use physical money to buy something.
example: Do you want to pay cash or use a card?

definition: To deal with the bad consequences of one's actions.
example: He didn't study, and now he has to pay the price.

definition: Freedom from disturbance, war, or violence.
An electronic device used for communication.

meaning: Textbook: Idiom for a very easy task. Spoken Native: While still common, it is often replaced in younger spoken slang by words like 'breeze' or 'walk in the park.'
definition: [object Object]
example: The exam was a piece of cake; I finished early.

definition: Showing good manners and respect for others.

meaning: Textbook: Adjective describing something fast. Spoken Native: Frequently used as a flat adverb instead of 'quickly' in conversational phrases (e.g., 'come here quick').
definition: Textbook: Adjective describing something fast. Spoken Native: Frequently used as a flat adverb instead of 'quickly' in conversational phrases (e.g., 'come here quick').
example: I need to run to the store quick before it closes.

definition: Making little or no noise; a person who speaks very little.
A person connected by blood or marriage.
Free from tension and anxiety.
No longer feeling distressed or anxious.
A hard, flat surface for vehicles, people, and animals to travel on.
The covering that forms the top of a building or vehicle.
A part or division of a building enclosed by walls.
Not polite; offensive or bad-mannered.
Content or pleased with what has been done or what has happened.
To avoid using too much effort or power.
example: Turn off the lights when you leave to save energy.

definition: To keep money for future use.
example: He is trying to save money for a new car.

definition: To rescue someone from dying.
example: The doctors worked hard to save his life.

definition: Caring only about oneself and not about other people.
Not joking; demanding careful consideration.

meaning: Textbook: Future action or obligation for first-person. Spoken Native: Rarely used except in formal documents or specific polite suggestions (e.g., 'Shall we dance?'); replaced by 'will' or 'should'.
definition: Textbook: Future action or obligation for first-person. Spoken Native: Rarely used except in formal documents or specific polite suggestions (e.g., 'Shall we dance?'); replaced by 'will' or 'should'.
example: We should head out soon before the traffic gets bad.

definition: Surprised and upset.

meaning: Textbook: Expresses recommendation or moral duty. Spoken Native: Often used to express high probability or expectation (e.g., 'The package should arrive today').
definition: Textbook: Expresses recommendation or moral duty. Spoken Native: Often used to express high probability or expectation (e.g., 'The package should arrive today').
example: If you sent the letter yesterday, they should receive it tomorrow.

definition: The joint connecting the arm to the body.
Nervous or uncomfortable around other people.
The natural outer layer covering the body.
The resting state in which the body is not active and the mind is unconscious.
Moving or operating at a low speed.
example: You should drive slow on these icy roads.

definition: The spiritual part of a person that some people believe continues to exist after death.
The organ in the body where food is digested.
Someone you do not know.
Experiencing mental or emotional pressure.
Demanding that rules concerning behavior are obeyed.

meaning: Textbook: Uncountable noun referring to raw material. Spoken Native: A generic word used to refer to a vague collection of objects, ideas, chores, or activities.
definition: Unspecified matter, objects, or content
example: I have a lot of stuff to take care of this weekend.

definition: To follow someone's guidance or suggestions.
example: You should take his advice; he knows what he's doing.

definition: To happen or occur.
example: The concert will take place in the park.

definition: To require a long period of time to achieve.
example: Learning a new language takes time.

definition: Fond of talking a lot.
Extremely bad or serious.

meaning: Textbook: Third-person plural pronoun. Spoken Native: The standard, natural way to refer to a single person whose gender is unknown or irrelevant.
definition: Textbook: Third-person plural pronoun. Spoken Native: The standard, natural way to refer to a single person whose gender is unknown or irrelevant.
example: Someone left their keys on the table; I hope they come back for them.

definition: The soft organ inside the mouth used for tasting, swallowing, and speaking.

meaning: Textbook: Metric unit of weight. Spoken Native: Used hyperbolically in conversation to mean a very large amount or quantity.
definition: Textbook: Metric unit of weight. Spoken Native: Used hyperbolically in conversation to mean a very large amount or quantity.
example: I have tons of emails to catch up on after my vacation.

definition: One of the hard white objects in the mouth used for biting and chewing.
The real facts about something, not a lie.
Unhappy, disappointed, or worried.
A solid structure that forms the side of a room or building.
A state of armed conflict between different countries or groups.
The clear liquid that has no color, taste, or smell, that falls from clouds as rain.
A period of seven days.

meaning: Textbook: Used as the objective pronoun of 'who'. Spoken Native: Replaced by 'who' in almost all spoken contexts (e.g., 'Who did you call?' instead of 'Whom did you call?').
definition: Textbook: Used as the objective pronoun of 'who'. Spoken Native: Replaced by 'who' in almost all spoken contexts (e.g., 'Who did you call?' instead of 'Whom did you call?').
example: Who are you going to the movie with?
That is the company whose database was hacked.

definition: An opening in a wall or vehicle that lets in light and air, usually filled with glass.
Thinking about unpleasant things that have happened or might happen.

meaning: Textbook: Non-standard dialectal contraction. Spoken Native: Used widely in the American South and informal contexts to fill the gap of a plural second-person pronoun ('you all').
definition: Textbook: Non-standard dialectal contraction. Spoken Native: Used widely in the American South and informal contexts to fill the gap of a plural second-person pronoun ('you all').
example: Are y'all ready to order, or do you need a few more minutes?

definition: A period of 365 or 366 days.
